one_line_takeaway: A systematization paper that enumerates 38 MCP-specific threat categories grounded in protocol elements, grouped into five remediation-oriented classes and cross-walked to STRIDE and both OWASP LLM/Agentic Top 10s; its one original empirical result is that five current open-source MCP security tools jointly cover at most 18 of the 38 categories, leaving semantic, logic-drift, and observability threats unaddressed.
key_finding: The paper's own evaluation finding is a tooling gap: across the five representative open-source MCP security tools evaluated, the combined union covers at most 18 of the 38 threat categories, and seven categories (MCP-15 Preference Manipulation, MCP-20 Indirect Prompt Injection, MCP-22 Insecure Human-in-the-Loop Bypass, MCP-23 Consent/Approval Fatigue, MCP-35 Agent Logic Drift, MCP-36 Multi-Agent Context Hijacking, MCP-38 No Observability) are covered by no evaluated tool. These uncovered threats concentrate in Category I (Semantic), Category IV (Logic Drift), and Category V (Observability) - precisely the threats unique to MCP's agentic operating model. The framework cross-walk finding is that all 10 OWASP LLM and all 10 OWASP Agentic categories map to at least one MCP-38 threat, while MCP-38 also surfaces threats with no OWASP analogue (e.g., MCP-15 MPMA, MCP-16 Rug Pull, MCP-31 DNS Rebinding).
core_contribution: MCP-38 is a protocol-specific threat taxonomy for Model Context Protocol (MCP) systems consisting of 38 enumerated threat categories (MCP-01 through MCP-38), each grounded in a specific MCP protocol element (tool manifests, inputSchema fields, stdio/SSE transport, multi-server routing, OAuth flows, etc.). The taxonomy's own organizing contribution is: (1) a five-way risk grouping (Category I Semantic Manipulation & Poisoning, II Prompt Injection & Boundary Breaking, III Identity/Trust & Supply Chain, IV Access Control & Logic Drift, V Data Exfiltration & Resource Abuse) chosen so that each category maps to a distinct class of remediation/engineering response; (2) a per-threat cross-walk to STRIDE and to both OWASP LLM Top 10 (2025, LLM01-LLM10) and OWASP Top 10 for Agentic Applications (2026, ASI01-ASI10); and (3) an evaluation of how well existing open-source MCP security tools cover the taxonomy. The paper's central thesis is that MCP's 'semantic attack surface' (natural-language tool descriptions and schema fields interpreted by the LLM at inference time) is a structurally new attack class not adequately captured by prior frameworks. This is a taxonomy/systematization paper, not an experimental one: it introduces no new attack technique or defense system of its own; the individual threats are consolidated and cross-mapped from prior academic work, industry advisories, CVEs, and registry observations that it cites.
threat_model: Adversary who can inject or modify any text the LLM reads within an MCP session without touching executable application code: a malicious or compromised MCP server, a tool-manifest author, a poisoned external document/resource/API response, a rogue registry entry, or a network man-in-the-middle. Because tool selection and invocation are mediated entirely by free-form natural-language descriptions interpreted by the LLM at inference time, such an adversary can steer tool selection (description/schema poisoning), inject instructions via retrieved data (indirect prompt injection), chain benign tools into exfiltration paths (parasitic toolchains), impersonate servers (no mandatory cryptographic identity), silently swap behavior post-audit (rug pull), or exfiltrate data via JSON-RPC payloads that evade network DLP. Assumed absent protections: MCP has no protocol-mandated mutual authentication, no manifest signing/integrity verification, no enforced instruction-data boundary, and no built-in audit trail. The privileged position of the agent itself (acting with the host user's permissions) is what turns semantic influence into real compromise, bypassing traditional sandbox isolation.
limitations: (1) Non-empirical / no new measurement: MCP-38 introduces no novel attack or defense and reports no attack-success-rate experiments of its own; all threat evidence is aggregated from cited third-party CVEs, advisories, PoCs, and blog/registry sources, so quoted figures (e.g., 30% SSRF, 33% unrestricted network access, MSB/MCPLib attack counts) are secondary citations, not this paper's results. (2) Version 1.0 self-labeled and dated March 2026; frameworks it maps to (OWASP Agentic 2026) are themselves new/moving targets. (3) Evidence base leans heavily on vendor blogs and community registry reports (Docker, Merge.dev, Reco AI, CyberArk, Netskope, etc.) of varying rigor, and the 'real-world incident' filter accepts controlled-lab PoCs as validation. (4) The five-category grouping and STRIDE/OWASP mappings are analyst judgments; the paper itself notes some threats (e.g., MCP-16 Rug Pull) map to multiple OWASP categories, so mappings are not always one-to-one. (5) The tool-coverage evaluation covers only five tools and reports coverage as a binary per-category judgment (no severity weighting or false-negative testing). (6) No inter-rater reliability, no formal completeness argument beyond 'every normative spec element examined.'

datasets_used: No conventional ML dataset; the taxonomy is derived from the MCP specification plus four tiers of evidence sources cited from prior work: NIST NVD CVE database (e.g., CVE-2025-6514 mcp-remote command injection); published security advisories (GitHub MCP Data Heist / Docker 'MCP Horror Stories' report); security-research PoCs (Guo et al. MCPLib; MCP Security Bench MSB); and MCP registry observations (Smithery, Glama)
quantitative_results: 38 threat categories enumerated (MCP-01 through MCP-38), grouped into 5 risk categories (I-V) [paper's own taxonomy]
Union of 5 evaluated open-source MCP security tools covers at most 18 of 38 taxonomy categories [paper's own evaluation, Section 5.2]
7 categories covered by no evaluated tool: MCP-15, MCP-20, MCP-22, MCP-23, MCP-35, MCP-36, MCP-38 [paper's own evaluation]

Per-tool coverage (Table 5): Enkrypt AI Scanner covers MCP-07/08/10/33 (34 uncovered); MCP-Scan/Invariant covers MCP-10/13/16/25 (34 uncovered); Akto Proxy covers MCP-07/08/24/25 (34 uncovered); Promptfoo Proxy covers MCP-04/24/25 (35 uncovered); AI-Infra-Guard/Tencent covers MCP-07/37 (36 uncovered)
Framework cross-walk: all 10 OWASP LLM Top 10 (LLM01-LLM10) and all 10 OWASP Agentic (ASI01-ASI10) categories map to >=1 MCP-38 threat [paper's own Table 4]

CITED (not this paper's own results): Merge.dev reports up to 30% of tested MCP servers vulnerable to SSRF (used in MCP-09); Docker reports 33% of tools allow unrestricted network access (used in MCP-32); MCPLib/Guo et al. provide 31 PoC attacks; MSB reports 12 attack vectors (8 core + 7 mixed)
baselines_compared: OWASP Top 10 for LLM Applications (2025), LLM01-LLM10 - rated Component-level, Low MCP specificity, Partial coverage in Table 1
OWASP Top 10 for Agentic Applications (2026), ASI01-ASI10 - Agent-level, Medium specificity, Partial coverage
MITRE ATT&CK - System-level, None MCP specificity, Indirect coverage
NIST AI RMF 1.0 - Governance-level, None specificity, no operational threat enumeration

MCP Security Bench (MSB) taxonomy of 12 MCP attack vectors, and prior MCP taxonomy literature (Hou et al.; Guo et al.; Jing et al.) used as cross-mapping references to position MCP-38 as a unifying superset
